items.q: What did Rutherford's alpha-scattering experiment show about the atom?
items.correct: Most of the atom is empty space, with a tiny dense positive nucleus.
items.misconception: It showed that atoms are solid spheres of positive charge with electrons embedded inside.
items.random_wrong: It showed that alpha particles can pass through any material because they are massless.
items.why: Students confuse the plum-pudding model (which Rutherford disproved) with the conclusion of his experiment.

items.q: Which type of nuclear radiation is most ionising?
items.correct: Alpha radiation — large, doubly charged particles cause the most ionisation per unit distance.
items.misconception: Gamma is most ionising because it can travel furthest and pass through almost anything.
items.random_wrong: Beta radiation is most ionising because it consists of fast electrons.
items.why: Students conflate penetrating power with ionising power — gamma penetrates most because it ionises least, the opposite of intuition.

items.q: When you rub a balloon and stick it to the wall, what has been transferred?
items.correct: Electrons have been transferred between the balloon and the cloth (not the wall).
items.misconception: Protons jumped from the balloon to the wall, causing static cling.
items.random_wrong: Charge was created by friction and pulled the balloon to the wall.
items.why: Students don't know that nuclei (and protons) are essentially immobile in everyday situations — only electrons move in static electricity.

items.q: A nurse stands near a gamma source for 5 minutes, then leaves. This is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Irradiation. “Contamination” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did the alpha scattering experiment show?
items.correct: Atoms are mostly empty space with a tiny positive nucleus
items.why: It's Atoms are mostly empty space with a tiny positive nucleus. “Electrons have negative charge” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A bone contains 1/8 of the C-14 of a fresh sample. C-14 has a half-life of about 5700 years. Roughly how old is the bone, in years?
items.why: Exact answer: 17100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
